evidence_quotes.quote: Digital games are increasingly being rendered unplayable by companies that shut down servers, revoke licenses, or discontinue DRM supportdespite players having legally purchased them.

paragraphs.text: Digital games are increasingly being rendered unplayable by companies that shut down servers, revoke licenses, or discontinue DRM supportdespite players having legally purchased them. This practice erodes consumer rights and sets a dangerous precedent where ownership means nothing beyond the lifespan of a publishers business decision. Many of these games hold artistic, cultural, and even historical value. When they are removed or disabled, entire communities and years of creativity are lost. The Digital Fairness Act must address this by enforcing digital preservation rights and ensuring games remain accessible, especially in single-player or peer-hosted formats, even after official support ends. Consumers deserve the right to access what they paid for. Killing games without offering alternatives or preservation options is not just unfairits digital vandalism. The law should require that publishers either maintain access or provide ways for the community to preserve or run the content independently. This issue is urgent. Every year, more games are being lost forever. We must act now to protect digital ownership and the integrity of our cultural media.
